skill.name: hooks-management
skill.description: Create, update, configure, and validate Claude Code hooks with event-specific input patterns and safety checks.
skill.summary: Manage Claude Code hook scripts, settings, input schemas, and validation workflows.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, environment, and blocker patterns, but review found they are mostly markdown examples and hook documentation. The skill is not malicious, but it helps create auto-running hooks that can execute commands and modify project hook settings, so users need a clear risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Auto-Executing Hook Creation Requires User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to create or update Claude Code hook scripts and validate their execution. Hooks can run automatically on events, so unsafe generated commands could modify files or affect a user session if accepted without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill goal and workflow explicitly cover creating hook scripts, and the reference warns that hooks execute arbitrary shell commands. The intent is legitimate automation, but the execution model has real security impact.

medium_findings.title: Environment Persistence Examples Could Encourage Secret Exposure
medium_findings.description: The reference includes SessionStart examples that write environment exports, including an API key placeholder, into the Claude environment file. The skill rules prohibit hardcoded credentials, but generated hooks should avoid copying this placeholder into real configurations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key value is a placeholder in documentation, not evidence of exfiltration. Risk remains because the skill may generate SessionStart hooks from these examples.

medium_findings.title: Prompt-Based Hooks Can Influence Agent Control Flow
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes prompt-based hook examples that can approve, block, or continue agent execution based on hook input. This is a supported feature, but generated prompts should be narrow and resistant to untrusted input inside hook arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference demonstrates prompt-based control decisions, but I found no malicious prompt injection text. The concern is a design risk when hook inputs are included in LLM prompts.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many Ruby backtick and shell command detections in markdown reference files. Review shows these are inline-code markers, JSON examples, or documented hook configuration fields rather than executable code shipped by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are markdown documentation, and the cited lines use prose or fenced examples. No standalone executable file or hidden command runner was found.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Sensitive File Hits Are Safety Guidance
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged path traversal and hidden file patterns. The reviewed lines describe hook input paths and explicitly instruct users to block traversal and skip sensitive files, so these particular hits are not malicious.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant references present sample input schemas and defensive best practices. The skill still works with file paths, so filesystem risk is retained separately as a medium operational concern.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Blocker Detections Lack Supporting Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections appear to be scanner matches against markdown text and hook examples. I did not find evidence of implemented cryptography, hashing misuse, or credential protection logic in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanned corpus is documentation and the reviewed areas do not implement crypto routines. Confidence is not perfect because the static report lists many weak-crypto hits without snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Auto-Executing Hook Creation Requires User Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is designed to create or update Claude Code hook scripts and validate their execution. Hooks can run automatically on events, so unsafe generated commands could modify files or affect a user session if accepted without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill goal and workflow explicitly cover creating hook scripts, and the reference warns that hooks execute arbitrary shell commands. The intent is legitimate automation, but the execution model has real security impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Persistence Examples Could Encourage Secret Exposure
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference includes SessionStart examples that write environment exports, including an API key placeholder, into the Claude environment file. The skill rules prohibit hardcoded credentials, but generated hooks should avoid copying this placeholder into real configurations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The API key value is a placeholder in documentation, not evidence of exfiltration. Risk remains because the skill may generate SessionStart hooks from these examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt-Based Hooks Can Influence Agent Control Flow
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes prompt-based hook examples that can approve, block, or continue agent execution based on hook input. This is a supported feature, but generated prompts should be narrow and resistant to untrusted input inside hook arguments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reference demonstrates prompt-based control decisions, but I found no malicious prompt injection text. The concern is a design risk when hook inputs are included in LLM prompts.

content.value_statement: Claude Code hooks are powerful but easy to misconfigure. This skill helps create, validate, and troubleshoot hook scripts using documented event patterns and security checks.
actual_capabilities: Creates and updates Claude Code hook scripts for supported hook events.
Uses documented hook input patterns for event-specific validation.
Reviews existing hooks under .claude/hooks and local settings files.
Tests hook behavior by piping sample JSON input into scripts.
Applies safety rules for credentials, infinite loops, and validation bypasses.
Produces a report after hook creation or troubleshooting.
limitations: It does not execute hooks safely by itself; the user environment determines runtime impact.
It depends on Claude Code hook behavior and settings format remaining compatible.
It cannot guarantee that every generated hook is safe without human review.
It is focused on Claude Code hooks, not general automation frameworks.

use_cases.title: Add a PreToolUse Guard
use_cases.description: Create a hook that checks risky Bash or file operations before Claude Code runs them.

use_cases.title: Debug Hook Input Handling
use_cases.description: Compare a failing hook against documented event input patterns and fix malformed parsing.

use_cases.title: Standardize Project Automation
use_cases.description: Set up consistent project hooks for formatting, validation, or notifications across a repository.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Existing Hooks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Claude Code hooks and settings. Explain what each hook does, when it runs, and any safety concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Validation Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PreToolUse hook that validates Bash commands. It should block risky patterns, handle invalid input, and return clear feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot a Hook Failure
prompt_templates.scenario: Fix a hook that does not run correctly
prompt_templates.prompt: Troubleshoot this Claude Code hook. Check the event input shape, settings matcher, script permissions, error handling, and test command.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Hook Dispatcher
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Python hook dispatcher for multiple Claude Code events. Include input validation, event routing, security checks, tests, and a rollout plan.

output_examples.input: User asks to review project hooks before enabling them.
output_examples.output: A concise audit listing each hook event, matched tools, expected action, required files, and security recommendations.

output_examples.input: User asks for a new command validation hook.
output_examples.output: A tested hook design with clear blocking behavior, malformed input handling, safe defaults, and setup instructions.

output_examples.input: User reports that a Stop hook loops indefinitely.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting report that identifies loop conditions, checks stop state handling, and recommends a corrected control flow.

best_practices: Review every hook command before enabling it in Claude Code settings.
Prefer Python hooks with explicit JSON parsing, path checks, and clear exit behavior.
Test hooks with representative event input before using them in active projects.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode credentials, tokens, or private paths in hook scripts.
Do not create Stop hooks that can continue execution without a termination condition.
Do not auto-approve broad tool patterns without narrow validation rules.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps create, update, validate, and troubleshoot Claude Code hooks for supported hook events.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill itself is documentation and workflow guidance, but generated hooks may run commands when enabled.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: Claude Code hooks can execute commands and access files, so safe use requires careful review and testing.

faq.question: Can it handle malformed hook input?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires generated hooks to parse JSON carefully and handle invalid input gracefully.

faq.question: Can it manage prompt-based hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. The references cover prompt-based hooks and decision outputs, with guidance to test prompts carefully.

faq.question: Should teams publish generated hooks directly?
faq.answer: No. Teams should review hook commands, test them in a safe environment, and document their purpose first.
